_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Generics practice 1
questions.prompt: Which declaration creates a list that accepts Integer values?
choices: List<Integer> scores = new ArrayList<>();
List<int> scores = new ArrayList<>();
List scores<Integer> = new ArrayList<>();
Integer<List> scores = new ArrayList<>();
questions.explanation: The element type belongs inside angle brackets after List. Collections use the wrapper type Integer rather than the primitive type int.

questions.title: Generics practice 2
choices: A type chosen when Box is used
A required class named T
The number of items in the box
A private text value
questions.explanation: T is a placeholder for a reference type. Declaring Box<String> replaces T with String for that use of the class.

questions.title: Trace Generics
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: This list accepts String values. Getting an item returns a String, so no cast is needed. The resulting output is 'CS101'.

questions.title: Generics good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Generics?
choices: Give every collection an element type.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Give every collection an element type. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Generics practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: The generic list rejects the wrong item as soon as the code is compiled. The raw list accepts it and may fail during a later cast.

questions.title: Generics practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Use reference types in angle brackets'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Write `Integer` instead of `int` and `Double` instead of `double`. Java automatically wraps and unwraps these values in most collection code.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Write `Integer` instead of `int` and `Double` instead of `double`. Java automatically wraps and unwraps these values in most collection code.

questions.title: Generics practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Generics?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Use wrapper types for primitive values.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use wrapper types for primitive values. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Generics practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Generics?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Avoid raw collection types.
questions.explanation: Avoid raw collection types. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Generics practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Generics?
choices: Let the diamond `<>` copy the type from the declaration.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Let the diamond `<>` copy the type from the declaration. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Generics practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Generics?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Use a type parameter when one design works for several types.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use a type parameter when one design works for several types. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
